presentation_metadata.title: Building Your First Agentic OS with OpenClaw

data.title: Introduction: The Evolution of the Agentic OS
data.sentence: Moving from 'question to answer' to 'goal to result'.
data.speaker_notes: Welcome, everyone, to 'Building Your First Agentic OS with OpenClaw.' We are currently witnessing a massive paradigm shift in how we interact with artificial intelligence. If we look at the evolution of AI interaction over the past couple of years, we started with simple, sandboxed chat applications. These chat models operated purely on a 'question to answer' basis, where you would ask a question, the AI would reply, and then you had to manually take that answer and do the actual work yourself.

data.title: The Paradigm Shift
bullets: Evolution from simple chat applications to advanced tool use
Expansion through Model Context Protocol (MCP) and custom skills
The Agentic OS: Goal-oriented instead of query-oriented
OpenClaw acts as a proactive entity, not a reactive chatbot
data.speaker_notes: From there, we evolved to more advanced applications that allowed expansion and customization through tools like Model Context Protocol (MCP) and custom skills. However, the ultimate evolution we are stepping into today is the 'Agentic OS'. An Agentic OS completely changes the dynamic from 'question to answer' to 'goal to result'. Instead of constantly ping-ponging prompts back and forth, you give the system a goal, and the agent proactively plans out the task, executes it, and delivers the final result.

data.title: A New Engineering Discipline

bullets: Crafting the perfect prompt
Manual execution of tasks
Iterative Q&A refinement
Context engineering for rich backgrounds
Incredibly simple daily prompts
Architecting and guiding automated systems

data.speaker_notes: This shift requires a new engineering discipline. We are moving away from traditional 'prompt engineering' and entering the era of 'agentic engineering'. In the past, people spent hours crafting the perfect prompt to get the right output. With an Agentic OS, you focus on 'context engineering,' where you load your agent with robust context about yourself, your business, and your preferences. Your daily job as a developer or operator changes from executing manual digital tasks to architecting and guiding these automated systems.

data.title: What is OpenClaw?
data.sentence: The harness that gives the AI brain a body.
data.speaker_notes: So, what exactly is OpenClaw? To understand it, we must break down the internal mechanism an agent uses to achieve its goals, which is known as the Agent Loop: Observe, Think, and Act.

data.title: The Core Mechanics

bullets: Observe: Gather context and research
Think: Formulate a plan for the next step
Act: Write code or execute tools
LLM serves as the brain (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models)
OpenClaw serves as the harness (hands, eyes, skills)

data.speaker_notes: When you give the agent a prompt, it doesn't just generate text; it continuously loops through three steps. First, it Observes by checking the workspace and gathering context. Second, it Thinks about what to do next and formulates a plan. Finally, it Acts by writing code or using tools. A helpful way to visualize this is the 'Brain in the Middle' model. The LLM acts as the 'brain,' but OpenClaw acts as the 'harness' or the body surrounding the brain, giving it hands, eyes, and an ecosystem of skills.

data.title: Channels & The Competitors Landscape
bullets: Seamless interaction via WhatsApp, Discord, Slack, and Telegram
Supports asynchronous voice memos
Alternative platforms: NemoClaw, Manus, Claude Code, Codeex
OpenClaw's edge: A generalized personal assistant that retains deep business context
data.speaker_notes: Furthermore, OpenClaw introduces seamless interaction through user-friendly channels. Instead of just typing commands into a terminal, you can interact with your agent using the chat apps you already use every day. You can even send it voice memos while you are driving. When we look at the competitors' landscape, there are other products taking a similar approach but in different ways... OpenClaw differentiates itself by acting as a generalized personal assistant that sits between you and all of your tools.

data.title: How to "Install" OpenClaw & Security Basics
data.sentence: Choosing your hardware and reducing the blast radius.
data.speaker_notes: Since our time is limited today, I will skip the basic step-by-step installation tutorial and jump straight into hardware choices, security advice, and how to configure an active system.

data.title: Hardware Choices & Security

bullets: Mac Mini: Local execution for top-tier privacy
VPS: Always-on, isolated, but exposes an SSH port
Raspberry Pi: Great for smart home integrations
Never run with root or admin accounts
Sandbox environments using Docker or restricted users
Apply the principle of least privilege

data.speaker_notes: When it comes to hardware, you have a few choices. You can run OpenClaw locally on a dedicated Mac Mini... Alternatively, you can run it on a VPS... No matter what hardware you choose, you must immediately implement core security practices to reduce the 'blast radius' if something goes wrong. The most crucial rule is to never run OpenClaw using a root or admin account. Always apply the principle of least privilege.

data.title: The Foundation Elements of OpenClaw
data.sentence: Onboarding your agent like a real human employee.
data.speaker_notes: Once installed, setting up OpenClaw is exactly like onboarding a real human employee. You cannot expect an executive assistant to walk into your office on day one and flawlessly execute tasks without understanding your business, your clients, and your tools. You must provide it with business context.

data.title: Identity Files
bullets: SOUL.md: Defines personality, core truths, values, and boundaries
IDENTITY.md: Defines the agent's name, vibe, and emojis
AGENTS.md: Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) rulebook and memory protocols
TOOLS.md: A reminder list of all accessible tools
data.speaker_notes: We do this using Identity Files, which define the agent. SOUL.md defines the agent's personality, core truths, values, and boundaries. IDENTITY.md is a minimal file defining who the agent is. AGENTS.md acts as your agent's Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) rulebook. TOOLS.md is a sticky note that reminds the agent of all the tools it has handy at its disposal.

data.title: Interaction Layers & Tooling

bullets: Tools: Typed functions for taking action
Skills: Markdown SOPs for step-by-step processes
Plugins: Community packages for models and integrations
Web Searching: Brave API, Grok, SearXNG
MCP: Standardizes connection to external apps (Gmail, Slack)

data.speaker_notes: OpenClaw interacts with the world using three primary layers: Tools, Skills, and Plugins. Tools are the typed functions the agent invokes to take action. Skills are Markdown files injected into the system prompt that act as SOPs. Plugins are packages published by the community. One essential capability is web searching, powered by Brave API, Grok, or SearXNG. To interact with your existing applications, we use the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which acts as a powerful universal translator.

data.title: Memory, Context & Reactivity
bullets: Context Compaction: Automatically compacts older conversations to save space
Daily Memory: Synthesizes important facts into a daily memory.md file
Segmentation: Use separate channels/threads for distinct tasks
Heartbeat: Internal cron job wakes up every 30 minutes to run background workflows
Obsidian Vault: Massive memory graph for Vector Search and RAG
data.speaker_notes: Managing memory and context is a critical aspect of an Agentic OS. OpenClaw automatically and continuously compacts older conversations to save space. Every night, it creates a daily memory.md file. A crucial best practice for memory management is segmenting your conversations into different channels. OpenClaw is also highly reactive thanks to its 'heartbeat', waking up every 30 minutes to proactively check tasks. Finally, you can elevate your agent's memory by integrating an Obsidian Vault for Vector Memory Search and RAG.

data.title: Best Practices & Live Demonstration
data.sentence: From unstructured vibe coding to repeatable logic.
data.speaker_notes: Now let's discuss best practices and look at a live demonstration. When building your Agentic OS, you must start simple. Start with a single agent handling a basic task. Move away from unstructured 'vibe coding' toward defining strict, repeatable, and logical processes.

data.title: The SWIFT Framework
bullets: Setup: Scope the project and connect API keys/MCPs
Workflow: Build the first draft manually with the agent
Iterate: Refine the output and prompt instructions
Formalize: Turn the perfected process into a repeatable .md skill
Trigger: Set to a cron schedule for automatic background execution
data.speaker_notes: Once you perfect a manual workflow in chat, move that workflow into a Skill. To systematically achieve this, we use the SWIFT Framework: Setup, Workflow, Iterate, Formalize, and Trigger. This turns perfected processes into repeatable skills and triggers them with cron schedules.

data.title: Auto-Improving the OS
bullets: LLM reviews codebase and logs daily
Identifies inefficiencies and proposes changes
Diagnoses and repairs broken workflows
Maintains the 'Human in the Loop' principle
data.speaker_notes: Your Agentic OS can also auto-improve. By leveraging the generative aspect of the LLM, you can tell OpenClaw to review its own codebase and conversation logs daily. It will analyze how a task was handled, identify inefficiencies, and propose code changes. And remember: shit happens. Keep a 'human in the loop,' but ask OpenClaw to solve its own problems by diagnosing log errors.

data.title: Security: Interacting with the Outside World Safely
data.sentence: Defending against prompt injections and malicious skills.
data.speaker_notes: As we integrate our agents with the real world, security is paramount. First, you must implement separate identities to reduce the blast radius.

data.title: Defensive Architecture

bullets: Prompt injections via emails and web pages
Hidden instructions to forward passwords
Malicious supply chain code in skills
Separate identities and isolated Agent Mail
Defensive text sanitation layers and frontier model scanning
Read-only access limits and draft approvals
Source code auditing before installing skills

data.speaker_notes: Do not let the agent operate using your personal browser profile. Create a distinct entity. You must understand that every input channel is a potential prompt injection surface. You must establish defensive text sanitation layers. Never blindly hand over the 'Keys to the Kingdom'. Limit the agent to read-only access wherever possible, verifying its work before approving destructive actions. Use Agent Mail for secure email isolation. Finally, always audit the source code of a third-party skill before installing it.

data.title: Closing Session
data.sentence: The true future we are building toward is Swarms.
data.speaker_notes: To wrap up, the ecosystem around Agentic AI is moving at a breakneck pace. OpenClaw is phenomenal, but it exists in a rapidly evolving landscape.

data.title: The Future: AI Swarms
bullets: Transitioning from a single-agent framework to a true Operating System
Orchestrating swarms of self-coordinating agents
Specialized agents handling marketing, coding, and finance simultaneously
The user acts as the human conductor of an entire AI workforce
data.speaker_notes: The true future we are building toward is Swarms. OpenClaw is transitioning from a framework that runs a single AI agent into a true Operating System that orchestrates entire swarms of self-coordinating agents. Soon, you won't just have an assistant; you will have an entire AI workforce reporting back to you, the human conductor.

data.quote: Thank you for your time. I'd now like to open the floor to any questions you might have.
data.speaker_notes: Thank you for your time. I'd now like to open the floor to any questions you might have.
